title: Listing Platform — System Design
description: Design a large-scale listing platform that ingests listings from external systems (FTP + API) and manual + AI-assisted authoring, runs them through validation, normalization, enrichment, deduplication, and fraud scoring, then publishes the survivors into a near-real-time search index for seekers on web and mobile. The core tension is operating a high-throughput, multi-tenant write pipeline behind a low-latency, always-available public read surface.

nodes.label: Listing Owner
nodes.description: Listing Owner: Company, agency, or individual who publishes listings.

nodes.label: External System
nodes.description: External System: Owner's ATS / CRM / DMS / inventory system pushing listings.

nodes.label: Seeker
nodes.description: Seeker: Public user searching for listings on web or mobile.

nodes.label: Platform Admin
nodes.description: Platform Admin: Internal operator running moderation, taxonomy, and policies.

nodes.label: Seeker Web / Mobile App
nodes.description: Seeker Web / Mobile App: Public seeker surface: website, mobile app, SEO landing pages.

nodes.label: Admin Panel
nodes.description: Admin Panel: Owner + platform-admin web app for listings, jobs, moderation, RBAC.

nodes.label: CDN / Edge Cache
nodes.description: CDN / Edge Cache: Caches seeker pages, listing detail JSON, and static media.

nodes.label: Public Search API
nodes.description: Public Search API: Read API for seeker search/detail/saved-search, fronted by CDN.

nodes.label: Owner / Partner API
nodes.description: Owner / Partner API: Authenticated write API for external systems: create/update/bulk/status.

nodes.label: Seeker BFF
nodes.description: Seeker BFF: Backend-for-frontend that composes search results, listing detail, and personalization.

nodes.label: SFTP Endpoint
nodes.description: SFTP Endpoint: Per-owner SFTP directories; landing zone for CSV/XML/JSON (incl. ZIP/GZIP) uploads.

nodes.label: Raw File Store
nodes.description: Raw File Store: Object storage holding raw uploaded files for audit, replay, and reprocessing.

nodes.label: File Watcher / Scheduler
nodes.description: File Watcher / Scheduler: Detects new SFTP files, registers ImportJob rows, and emits import events.

nodes.label: Stream Parser
nodes.description: Stream Parser: Streams CSV/XML/JSON row-by-row from object storage so 10M-row files don't OOM.

nodes.label: Schema Validator
nodes.description: Schema Validator: Per-row schema + business-rule validation; emits per-row pass/fail.

nodes.label: Listing Event Bus
nodes.description: Listing Event Bus: Kafka-style log partitioned by listing_id; carries every lifecycle transition.

nodes.label: Outbox / CDC
nodes.description: Outbox / CDC: Transactional outbox tailing the listing DB so downstreams never miss a state change.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Queue
nodes.description: Dead-Letter Queue: Holds poison rows/messages for inspection and replay.

nodes.label: Listing Store
nodes.description: Listing Store: Source of truth for listings, owners, versions, lifecycle state, and audit links.

nodes.label: Import & Audit Store
nodes.description: Import & Audit Store: ImportJob / ImportRecord / AuditLog rows; per-row error reports for owners.

nodes.label: Media / Image Store
nodes.description: Media / Image Store: Original and derived images/video for listings; CDN-fronted.

nodes.label: Normalization Worker
nodes.description: Normalization Worker: Canonicalizes fields (location, price, currency, units) and maps owner taxonomy to ours.

nodes.label: Enrichment Orchestrator
nodes.description: Enrichment Orchestrator: Drives the enrichment plan: classification, geocoding, language, quality scores.

nodes.label: ML Model Service
nodes.description: ML Model Service: Serves classification, embedding, translation, and image-analysis models.

nodes.label: Geocoding Service
nodes.description: Geocoding Service: Address -> normalized location + lat/lng; calls external provider with caching.

nodes.label: Duplicate Detection
nodes.description: Duplicate Detection: Exact + fuzzy dedup using external_id, content hash, and embedding similarity.

nodes.label: External-ID Map
nodes.description: External-ID Map: (owner_id, external_listing_id) -> listing_id index for deterministic dedup.

nodes.label: Content Hash & Embedding Index
nodes.description: Content Hash & Embedding Index: MinHash/SimHash + vector index for near-duplicate lookups (blocking + ANN).

nodes.label: Fraud Scoring Service
nodes.description: Fraud Scoring Service: Combines rules + ML signals into a fraud risk score; logs decisions.

nodes.label: Feature Store
nodes.description: Feature Store: Owner reputation, behavior counters, suspicious-IP features for fraud scoring.

nodes.label: Rules Engine
nodes.description: Rules Engine: Hot-deployable rule packs (banned phrases, regex, owner suspensions).

nodes.label: Moderator Review Queue
nodes.description: Moderator Review Queue: Holds-for-review queue for fraud-flagged and seeker-reported listings.

nodes.label: AI Assistance Service
nodes.description: AI Assistance Service: LLM-backed: title/desc generation, category suggestion, policy detection, translation.

nodes.label: Search Indexer
nodes.description: Search Indexer: Consumes outbox/event bus, projects listings into SearchDocs, writes to the index.

nodes.label: Search Index (OpenSearch)
nodes.description: Search Index (OpenSearch): Sharded inverted + geo index with alias-swapped versions for blue/green reindexing.

nodes.label: Query / Detail Cache
nodes.description: Query / Detail Cache: Caches hot search results and listing-detail JSON for the seeker read path.

nodes.label: Identity & Access Mgmt
nodes.description: Identity & Access Mgmt: OAuth2, API keys, RBAC, owner-tenant scoping, mTLS for partner systems.

nodes.label: Rate Limiter
nodes.description: Rate Limiter: Per-owner / per-API-key quotas, burst control, abuse protection.

nodes.label: Webhook Dispatcher
nodes.description: Webhook Dispatcher: Signs and retries import-completion / status callbacks to owners.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Notification Service: Owner email/in-app alerts on validation errors, fraud holds, expiry, etc.

nodes.label: Analytics Pipeline
nodes.description: Analytics Pipeline: Captures seeker events (view/click/contact), feeds dashboards and ranking signals.

nodes.label: Observability (metrics/logs/traces)
nodes.description: Observability (metrics/logs/traces): Dashboards for indexing lag, import jobs, fraud decisions, DLQ depth, API per-owner usage.

functional: Ingest listings via SFTP (CSV/XML/JSON, possibly ZIP/GZIP), incrementally or in bulk.
Ingest listings via authenticated REST API: create, update, partial-update, delete, bulk.
Manual + AI-assisted listing authoring in the admin panel (title generation, category suggestion, policy checks, translation).
Validate, normalize, and enrich listings (geocoding, classification, quality/completeness scores) before publication.
Detect exact + near-duplicate listings across owners and channels before they reach search.
Score every listing for fraud; auto-publish below threshold, route flagged ones to a moderator queue.
Maintain a near-real-time search index supporting keyword, filter, geo, and ranking by relevance / freshness / quality.
Serve seekers on a public website and mobile app: search, detail pages, saved listings, alerts, report-listing.
Admin panel for owners (their listings + imports + analytics) and platform admins (moderation, taxonomy, RBAC, audits).
Owner-facing import status, error reports, and webhook callbacks for asynchronous completion.
nonFunctional: Public seeker search and listing detail are always-available: ingestion degradation must NOT take down the read surface.
Search index freshness ~5 minutes (a publish-to-visible target most listings beat).
Search query latency p95 < 300 ms; listing detail p95 < 200 ms.
Scale: 10M active / 500M historical listings, 50k owners, 5k FTP imports/day, ~100M API writes/month, 50M seeker searches/day.
At-least-once processing with idempotency: re-uploading the same file or replaying a message must not create duplicates.
Multi-tenant isolation: an owner's blast or bug can't starve other owners or break seeker reads.
Audit every state transition, fraud decision, and admin action; support replay of failed processing jobs.
GDPR-style deletion and PII protection for owner contact data.

capacity.label: Active listings
capacity.note: Indexed + serveable; the working set for seeker traffic.

capacity.label: Listing owners
capacity.note: Tenant scope for partitioning, quotas, and RBAC.

capacity.label: Ingestion writes
capacity.note: 5k FTP imports/day (some 10M-row files) + ~100M API writes/month + manual.

capacity.label: Pipeline work
capacity.note: Validate, normalize, enrich, dedup, fraud, index — each a partitioned worker pool.

capacity.label: Seeker reads
capacity.note: Peak ~10x average; heavily cacheable; CDN+QueryCache absorbs most.

capacity.label: Search freshness
capacity.note: Most listings beat that; flagged-for-review may take hours.

api.description: Create one listing. Idempotency-Key dedupes producer retries; the listing flows through the async pipeline before becoming visible.
api.request:
Idempotency-Key: req_42
Authorization: Bearer <token>
{
  "external_listing_id": "ext_9",
  "category": "jobs",
  "title": "Senior backend engineer",
  "description": "...",
  "location": "Berlin, DE",
  "salary": { "min": 80000, "currency": "EUR" },
  "media": [{ "url": "..." }]
}

api.description: Partial update; same async pipeline. Returns the new correlation id for status polling.
Bulk upload up to N listings. Always asynchronous — returns a job id the partner polls or gets via webhook.
Status of one import job: counts of total / succeeded / failed / duplicate, plus an error-report URL.
Current lifecycle state + last fraud / dedup / enrichment decision for one listing.
Public seeker search. Heavily cacheable (CDN + QueryCache).

dataModel.name: listings
dataModel.note: Source of truth. Owner-provided fields, enriched fields, and lifecycle state. Versioned (one row per version) so audit + rollback are trivial.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(draft, validated, enriched, dup_suspected, fraud_review, approved, published, paused, expired, rejected, deleted)

dataModel.name: listing_owners
dataModel.note: Tenant. Reputation, billing, and integration configuration live here.

fields.name: integrations
fields.type: jsonb (ftp config, api keys, webhook urls)

dataModel.name: import_jobs
dataModel.note: One row per FTP file or bulk-API submission. Counts roll up from import_records.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(received, parsing, validating, enriching, deduping, fraud_checking, indexed, partial_failed, failed)

dataModel.name: import_records
dataModel.note: Per-row outcome. Drives owner-facing error reports and partial-failure replay.

dataModel.name: enrichment_results
dataModel.note: Append-only history of enrichment outputs, separable from owner-provided fields.

fields.name: enrichment_type
fields.type: enum(category, geocode, language, translate, quality, completeness, seo, embedding)

dataModel.name: fraud_decisions
dataModel.note: One row per scoring run. Explains why a listing was held / cleared and supports backtesting rule changes.

dataModel.name: duplicate_candidates
dataModel.note: Pairs of suspected duplicate listings. Resolved into duplicate_group_id on the listing.

dataModel.name: external_id_map
dataModel.note: (owner_id, external_listing_id) -> listing_id. The deterministic-dedup table; a unique key here lets the platform absorb idempotent re-imports cheaply.

dataModel.name: audit_log
dataModel.note: Every state change, admin action, and override. Required for compliance and post-incident review.

patterns.name: Async pipeline on a partitioned event bus
patterns.what: Each pipeline stage is its own consumer group on a log partitioned by listing_id; stages scale and fail independently.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-stage processing where latency budget per write is seconds-to-minutes and stages have different cost/SLOs.

patterns.name: Outbox + CDC
patterns.what: Write the listing row and an outbox row in one transaction; a tail process publishes outbox rows to the event bus.
patterns.whenToUse: When downstream projections (search index, analytics) must never miss a state change, even if a producer crashes.

patterns.name: Idempotency key + deterministic dedup table
patterns.what: Per-owner unique (owner_id, external_listing_id) absorbs repeated imports of the same logical listing.
patterns.whenToUse: When the same listing arrives over FTP today and API tomorrow, or producers retry on timeouts.

patterns.name: Blocking + ANN for fuzzy dedup
patterns.what: Reduce O(N^2) comparisons by blocking on cheap keys (category + city), then near-neighbor search in an embedding/MinHash index.

patterns.name: Rules + ML fraud scoring
patterns.what: Hot-reloadable rule packs as a cheap filter; ML model contributes a probabilistic score; both feed one decision.
patterns.whenToUse: When you need fast rule rollouts AND ML quality; pure ML is too slow to iterate on emerging abuse.

patterns.name: Source-of-truth + projected search index
patterns.what: Listing DB is authoritative; SearchIdx is a derived projection rebuilt from the DB via blue/green alias swap.
patterns.whenToUse: When search shape changes more often than the truth, or a bad indexer needs a clean recovery path.

patterns.name: CDN + cache-aside read path
patterns.what: Public search and detail responses are CDN-edge cached; misses go through a query cache, then the search index / DB.
patterns.whenToUse: When read traffic is orders of magnitude larger than writes and most queries are repeats.

patterns.name: Per-tenant partitioning + fair scheduling
patterns.what: Partition the event bus by owner_id so one noisy owner can't starve others; a fair scheduler weights consumer capacity across tenants.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tenant write pipelines with skewed owner sizes.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Synchronous Create-and-Index (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that could possibly work: the partner POSTs a listing, the API validates it, runs enrichment + fraud + dedup inline, writes the listing row, and pushes a document into the search index — all in one synchronous request before returning 201.

It's correct and easy to reason about, and it exposes every pressure this design needs to absorb: a slow ML model or geocoder couples directly to ingestion latency, a 10M-row FTP file is impossible because we have no async path, an indexer outage takes down writes, and a single noisy partner can saturate the API for everyone.
Every later step removes one of these couplings.

view.caption: Everything happens inline: validate, score, dedup, write, index — then return. Simple, and a single slow downstream blocks the whole request path.

steps.decisionPrompt: If geocoding takes 800ms and ML fraud scoring takes 200ms, what is your minimum POST latency — and what happens when either is unavailable?
whyNow: A concrete baseline makes the rest of the design earn its complexity step-by-step.

concepts.term: Coupling vs cohesion at the request boundary
concepts.definition: Whether the caller's success depends on every downstream succeeding right now, or just on the system accepting the work for eventual processing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Synchronous create-and-index couples partner availability to your slowest pipeline stage. The next step decouples it.

traps.trap: Shipping the naive design and adding async 'later'
traps.why: FTP imports of 10M rows are simply not possible synchronously — the architecture must be async from day one.
traps.instead: Treat the API as 'accept fast, process async' from the start; every later step is just adding pipeline stages.

recap.after: End-to-end create works, in one request.
recap.newRisk: Latency, availability, and throughput all collapse the moment a downstream is slow or the file is large.

steps.title: 2. API Ingestion: Accept Fast, Publish to an Event Bus
description: Turn the API into a thin accept layer. The Owner API authenticates (OAuth2 / API key / mTLS), rate-limits per owner, validates the request shape, claims an idempotency key, publishes a `listing.upserted` event to a partitioned log, and returns 202 immediately with a correlation id.
All the real work moves behind the bus. A slow downstream now means the event sits in the log, not that the partner's HTTP call times out.
Idempotency-Key on POST + (owner_id, external_listing_id) lookup on the consumer side ensures retries don't create duplicate listings.

view.caption: The API authenticates, validates, and idempotently publishes one event per logical write. Webhooks fire async on terminal status.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the smallest unit of work the API has to do synchronously, and what's the smallest contract the bus needs to enforce idempotency?

concepts.definition: The Idempotency-Key header dedupes producer retries of the same HTTP request; the (owner_id, external_listing_id) tuple dedupes the underlying logical listing across requests/channels.
concepts.whyItMatters: Producers retry on network errors; partners re-upload yesterday's full file 'just to be safe'. Both must collapse to one listing.

concepts.definition: Token-bucket counters keyed by owner_id (or API key) capping writes/sec and concurrent in-flight jobs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Multi-tenant write surfaces fail by tenant first: one buggy partner shouldn't saturate your API for everyone else.

concepts.definition: An id the API returns and stamps on every downstream log/metric for this write.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, debugging an async pipeline is impossible — partners report 'my POST cor_77 never got published' and you grep all stages.

whyNow: Decoupling the API from processing is the foundation everything else depends on.

flows.name: API accept + idempotent publish
flows.note: Heavy work moves behind the bus; the API does auth + validate + claim + publish only.

strong: Separates accept from process and returns a correlation id.
Uses BOTH an idempotency key (producer retries) and a deterministic dedup tuple (logical listing).
Partitions the bus by owner_id so per-owner ordering is preserved.
weak: Runs validation 'after the queue' — schema/shape errors should still 4xx synchronously.
Uses only an idempotency key, missing the (owner_id, external_id) dedup that catches FTP+API double-ingest.

traps.trap: Returning 200 OK with a fake 'created' status
traps.why: The listing isn't visible yet; partners think it's live and ask why search doesn't return it.
traps.instead: Return 202 Accepted with a correlation id + status endpoint, so partners can poll or wait on a webhook.

recap.before: Everything inline on the request path.
recap.after: API accepts fast and idempotently; processing happens behind the bus.
recap.newRisk: The pipeline stages downstream of the bus now need to exist — and not lose messages.

steps.title: 3. FTP Ingestion: Stream Large Files, Don't Load Them
description: Partners drop files into per-owner SFTP directories. A file watcher detects new arrivals, registers an `import_jobs` row, copies the raw file into object storage (the audit trail), and emits `import.received`.

A streaming parser reads the file row-by-row from object storage — never into memory — and emits one `row.parsed` event per row onto the same event bus the API uses. Malformed rows go to a DLQ with the row number + raw bytes; malformed files fail the whole job and notify the owner.
Re-uploading the same file is safe because the per-row events flow into the same `(owner_id, external_listing_id)` dedup as the API path.

view.caption: Files land in object storage; a streaming parser fans them into row events on the same bus the API uses. Per-row failures DLQ; the rest flow through.

steps.decisionPrompt: A partner uploads a 10M-row, 4 GB CSV. Where does it live, and what's the smallest unit of work your pipeline acts on?

concepts.definition: An object-storage bucket holding the original file forever (or for a long retention window).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you replay the whole job after a parser bug fix, and gives auditors the bytes the partner actually sent.

concepts.definition: The pipeline's unit of work is one row, not one file. Failed rows DLQ; succeeded rows flow through.
concepts.whyItMatters: Partial failures shouldn't block successful rows — owners want 99,840 of 100,000 published, not zero.

concepts.definition: Owner config declares whether a file represents a full snapshot (missing listings get expired) or a delta (only the rows present change).
concepts.whyItMatters: Same file shape, two very different behaviors — getting this wrong silently expires a partner's whole catalog.

whyNow: FTP is the bulk path; once API is async, FTP is 'turn each row into the same event'.

flows.name: Streaming file ingestion
flows.note: One file -> N row events. The parser never holds the whole file in memory.

options.name: Streaming parser (default)
pros: Bounded memory regardless of file size — a 10M-row file is the same shape as a 100-row file.
Per-row failures don't poison the rest of the job.
Same row event as the API path: one downstream pipeline, two ingress channels.
cons: More moving parts than 'load and process'.
Per-row event overhead at small file sizes.

options.name: Load entire file, process in memory
cons: OOMs on large files; entire job retries the whole file.
No partial progress — 9,999,999 successful rows are lost if row 10M fails.

failureDrills.scenario: Partner re-uploads yesterday's full file with no changes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Zero new listings are created; each row hits the existing (owner_id, external_id) entry and becomes a no-op update.
failureDrills.mitigation: Deterministic dedup table enforces uniqueness; downstream stages skip work when nothing changed (content hash unchanged).

failureDrills.scenario: Parser crashes mid-file after 8M of 10M rows.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Parser resumes from a checkpoint (row offset) on restart; previously emitted rows are idempotent.
failureDrills.mitigation: Parser commits a row-offset checkpoint per N rows; on restart it re-reads from the offset and emits — downstream dedup absorbs the overlap.

strong: Streams from object storage, never loads the file into memory.
Treats the row as the unit of work and routes per-row failures to a DLQ.
Uses the SAME event bus + dedup tuple as the API path — one pipeline, two ingress channels.
weak: Reads the whole file in one process and 'retries the file' on partial failure.
Builds a separate FTP-only pipeline parallel to the API pipeline.

traps.trap: Letting one bad row fail the whole job
traps.why: Partners want partial success — a typo in row 7 shouldn't block rows 8 through 10M.
traps.instead: DLQ the bad row, surface it in the per-row error report, and keep going (unless the owner asked for all-or-nothing).

recap.after: Bulk FTP at any file size, joining the same row-event pipeline as the API.
recap.newRisk: Bus throughput now matters; downstream stages must absorb peaks from one 10M-row drop.

steps.title: 4. Async Pipeline: Normalize → Enrich → Persist
description: Each pipeline stage is its own consumer group on the event bus, partitioned by listing_id. The Normalizer canonicalizes fields (currency, units, owner taxonomy → ours) and writes the first listing row in state `validated`. The Enrichment orchestrator then calls geocoding, classification, language detection, translation, and quality scoring, writing each result into `enrichment_results` and updating the listing.

Owner-provided fields and enrichment outputs are kept separable — the listing schema marks every enriched field with its source and model version, so a bad model rollout never silently overwrites partner data.
Enrichment failures are classified: transient (retry with backoff), permanent (DLQ + notify owner), or non-blocking (publish anyway with `enrichment.partial`).

view.caption: Stages compose on the bus. Each writes its own slice; the listing row carries owner-provided AND enriched fields, with provenance.

steps.decisionPrompt: Geocoding takes 600ms p95 and the ML category model takes 200ms. Do you run them in parallel, sequentially, or batch them?
patterns: Async pipeline on a partitioned event bus

concepts.term: Pipeline stage as a consumer group
concepts.definition: Each stage subscribes to the bus and emits its own output topic; stages scale workers independently based on their lag.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enrichment is much slower than normalization. If they shared a worker pool, the slow stage would starve the fast one.

concepts.definition: Every enriched field carries (source = enricher, model_version, confidence, enriched_at).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you A/B model versions, reprocess after a model bug, and never accidentally overwrite an owner's value with a model guess.

concepts.definition: Transient retries with backoff; permanent dead-letters with a reason; non-blocking proceeds with partial enrichment and logs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without this split, a flaky geocoder either takes down ingestion (over-retrying) or silently kills listings (DLQ everything).

whyNow: Once events flow, the real value-add work — enrichment — gets its own scaling and failure budgets.

deepDives.title: Enrichment orchestration
points: Plan: a config-driven DAG of (geocode → classify → translate → quality) per listing type.
Parallelism: run independent enrichers in parallel; serial only when one feeds another (translation depends on language detection).
Reprocessing: when a model ships, kick off a backfill consumer that re-emits affected listings into the enrichment topic without disturbing live writes.
Cost control: cache geocoding by normalized address; cache LLM outputs by content hash; degrade gracefully when budgets exceeded.

deepDives.title: AI-assisted authoring (admin panel)
points: Same model service serves both async enrichment AND interactive AI suggestions in the admin UI (title generation, category suggestion, policy detection).
Interactive calls have stricter latency budgets and stream tokens; async calls batch.
Every AI suggestion the owner accepts is logged with model + prompt version for audit — a hallucinated salary that goes live is a real lawsuit.

options.name: Orchestrator with parallel sub-calls (default)
pros: Easy to add/remove enrichers via config.
cons: Partial-failure semantics need explicit policy per enricher.

options.name: Choreography: each enricher subscribes independently
pros: No orchestrator; enrichers scale fully independently.
cons: Race conditions writing the same listing row from N stages.
Hard to know when 'enrichment is done' (no single completion signal).

strong: Separates pipeline stages so each scales / fails independently.
Keeps owner-provided fields distinct from enrichment outputs with provenance.
Has an explicit policy for each enrichment failure class.
weak: Writes enrichment results back into owner-provided fields with no provenance.
Treats every enrichment failure the same way (always DLQ, or always retry forever).

traps.trap: Sequential enrichment so 'enrich' becomes one long step
traps.why: Latency = sum of all enricher latencies; one slow vendor stalls every listing.
traps.instead: Parallel calls when independent; serial only on real data dependencies.

traps.why: A bad model rollout silently overwrites owner data; rolling back means restoring from backups.
traps.instead: Mark every enriched field with model_version + source; rollback is 'reprocess with the previous version'.

recap.before: Row events with nothing acting on them.
recap.after: A normalized, enriched listing row in the DB with separable provenance and a CDC outbox.
recap.newRisk: Duplicates haven't been merged yet, and nothing has scored these for fraud.

steps.title: 5. Duplicate Detection: Exact First, Fuzzy Second
description: Two layers of deduplication run before publication. Exact dedup is a unique-key lookup in the external-ID map keyed by `(owner_id, external_listing_id)` — this absorbs the most common case (a partner re-pushes the same listing from FTP and API) at lookup cost.

Fuzzy dedup catches the harder cases: a listing posted by two different owners, a reposted listing with minor edits, the same job description from an aggregator. A content hash on normalized fields blocks the obvious clones; a vector embedding + ANN index over (title + description + city + price band) catches near-duplicates without N-squared comparisons.
Both layers feed a moderator review queue when confidence is borderline. Merge decisions are auditable and reversible: the system writes a `duplicate_group_id`, not a hard merge.

view.caption: Exact dedup is a hash lookup; fuzzy dedup is blocking + ANN. Borderline matches route to a human queue; merges record a group, not a destructive merge.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you stop a 10M-listing fuzzy-dedup run from doing 10M × 10M comparisons?

concepts.definition: Deterministic uses a stable per-owner external id (exact match, unique constraint). Fuzzy uses content signals (hashes, embeddings, geography) when there's no shared id.
concepts.whyItMatters: 90% of duplicates are caught by deterministic dedup at trivial cost. The fuzzy layer earns its complexity on the remaining hard cases.

concepts.definition: Reduce the comparison space by first bucketing candidates on cheap keys (category + city + price band) before running the expensive similarity comparisons.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without blocking, fuzzy dedup is O(N^2) and intractable. With blocking, it's O(N * avg_bucket_size).

concepts.definition: Listings keep their identity; the system groups duplicates by a shared id. The search index publishes the canonical representative; everything else is hidden but recoverable.
concepts.whyItMatters: A false merge is unrecoverable if you actually overwrote the loser. Grouping makes 'oops, those weren't duplicates' a one-row update.

whyNow: Dedup must run BEFORE indexing — duplicates in search results are the most visible quality problem.

flows.name: Two-layer dedup
flows.note: Cheap deterministic first; fuzzy ANN with blocking second; humans only on borderline.

strong: Exact dedup with a unique key on (owner_id, external_id) for idempotent re-imports.
Blocking strategy before any embedding/ANN comparisons.
Reversible grouping (duplicate_group_id) instead of destructive merges.
Human-in-the-loop for borderline scores.
weak: Full O(N^2) similarity scan with no blocking.
Destructive merge with no audit / undo path.
Auto-merging across different owners without explicit policy.

traps.why: Two real-estate agencies legitimately list the same building; merging deletes one owner's listing.
traps.instead: By default only auto-merge WITHIN an owner; cross-owner duplicates go to a moderator with both records visible.

traps.trap: Doing fuzzy dedup synchronously on every write
traps.why: An ANN lookup over 10M vectors is too slow for the write path; it'd dominate ingest latency.
traps.instead: Run fuzzy dedup as its own pipeline stage with its own backpressure — bounded latency, not bounded write latency.

failureDrills.scenario: A bug marks 1M listings as duplicates incorrectly.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reversible. Admin can bulk-clear duplicate_group_id and reprocess; nothing was destroyed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Grouping (not merging) + audit of every dedup decision; replay tooling that rewrites group ids based on a fresh dedup model.

recap.before: Duplicates leaked into the listing store.
recap.after: Same-owner re-imports collapse for free; cross-owner near-duplicates are scored and grouped, with humans for borderline.
recap.newRisk: Fraudulent listings still get through — dedup recognizes 'this is the same listing', not 'this is a scam'.

steps.title: 6. Fraud Prevention: Rules + ML, with a Human Loop
description: Every listing that survives dedup gets scored. A rules engine evaluates hot-deployable rule packs (banned phrases, regex, suspicious-IP, banned owner, payment scam phrases) first — cheap, explainable, and editable by trust-and-safety without a deploy. An ML scoring service then adds a probabilistic risk score using features from a feature store: owner reputation, recent listing churn, image-reuse signals, content embeddings.

Listings below a safe threshold auto-publish. Above a high threshold, auto-reject + notify owner. In the middle band, hold for moderator review with the explanation (which rules fired, which features contributed). Moderator decisions feed back into the ML training set and into owner-reputation features.
Every fraud decision is logged with model version and triggered rules so false-positive / false-negative rates can be measured per cohort.

view.caption: Cheap rules first, expensive ML second, humans last. Every decision logged with triggered_rules + model_version for auditability.

steps.decisionPrompt: What do you do when the ML fraud service is unavailable? Block publication, or publish-and-flag?

concepts.definition: Rules are explicit, instant to deploy, fully explainable. ML is probabilistic, slower to iterate, but catches patterns rules can't enumerate. The platform combines both into one score.
concepts.whyItMatters: Pure ML is too slow to iterate against adversaries who change tactics weekly. Pure rules can't catch novel patterns. The combination wins.

concepts.definition: Pre-computed, low-latency features (owner reputation rolling avg, recent listing churn, image-reuse fingerprint hits) read by the scoring service at sub-10ms.
concepts.whyItMatters: ML scoring needs features fast; computing them in the request path means seconds-per-listing latency.

concepts.definition: Every score persists with the rules that fired, the model version, and the features that contributed most.
concepts.whyItMatters: Owners ask 'why was my listing held?'; auditors require it; ML training needs labeled data — explanation handles all three.

concepts.definition: When fraud service is unavailable, default behavior: publish low-risk owners, hold the rest. Documented and tunable.
concepts.whyItMatters: An adversary could deliberately overload the scorer to bypass it; the fallback must not be 'open the floodgates'.

whyNow: Bad listings are the worst possible search result — fraud must run before the search index sees the listing.

failureDrills.scenario: ML fraud service is down for 30 minutes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Rules-only mode kicks in: low-risk owners publish, anything that triggers a rule holds. Backlog drains when ML returns.
failureDrills.mitigation: Hard fallback policy documented; circuit breaker on ML calls; ops alerted on degraded-mode entry.

failureDrills.scenario: A new banned-phrase rule incorrectly holds 5% of legitimate listings.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Trust-and-safety hot-reverts the rule pack; held listings are auto-released without re-running the full pipeline.
failureDrills.mitigation: Rule packs versioned and hot-deployable; auto-release tooling rescans held listings against the new active rule set.

strong: Combines hot-reloadable rules with ML scoring, with rules running first as a cheap filter.
Logs decision + triggered rules + model version for every listing.
Has an explicit degradation policy when ML is unavailable.
Closes the loop: moderator decisions feed training data + owner reputation.
weak: Pure ML with no explainability or rule layer.
Synchronous fraud scoring on the API write path.
Hard-fails publication when fraud service is down (or — worse — silently publishes everything).

traps.trap: Tuning the threshold without measuring false positives
traps.why: Cranking the threshold catches more fraud — and hides 30% of legitimate listings owners stop paying for.
traps.instead: Track FP/FN rates per owner cohort; report them to T&S as part of every rule change.

recap.after: Every listing is scored; bad ones go to humans with explanations, not to seekers.
recap.newRisk: Approved listings still need to reach the search index quickly without coupling to the write path.

steps.title: 7. Search Indexing: Project the Listing DB into the Search Index
description: Listing DB is the source of truth; the search index is a derived projection. A transactional outbox writes one row per state change in the same transaction as the listing update, and a tail process publishes those rows to the event bus. The indexer consumes from the bus and upserts/deletes SearchDocs in the search cluster.

Reindexing is a first-class operation: spin up a new index with the new schema, replay all `published` listings into it (from the DB, not the bus), then alias-swap reads onto the new index. Old index stays around for instant rollback.
The indexer is partitioned by listing_id so per-listing order is preserved; index writes are batched + flushed on a small timer to keep throughput high without spiking commit lag.

view.caption: Listing DB is the truth; Outbox + CDC guarantees nothing is lost; the indexer projects DB state into a sharded search index. Reindex via blue/green alias swap.

steps.decisionPrompt: A listing is updated in the DB but never reaches the search index. What was the bug, and what pattern prevents it?

concepts.definition: Write the entity + a row to an outbox table in the same DB transaction; a tail process publishes outbox rows to the bus, exactly mirroring committed state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, you either dual-write (lose events on crash between writes) or rely on the indexer to scan the DB (slow + bursty).

concepts.definition: Build a new index version in the background, then atomically swap the read alias from the old to the new index.
concepts.whyItMatters: Schema changes and full reindexes happen without read downtime; rollback is one alias swap.

concepts.definition: Time from listing.published in DB to visible in search index — typically a few seconds at p50, ~minutes at p99.
concepts.whyItMatters: Owners and seekers feel lag concretely ('I published, why isn't it in search?'); SLO drives alerting and capacity planning.

whyNow: Once the pipeline produces approved listings, the read surface needs them visible — fast but not synchronously.

flows.name: Outbox-driven indexing
flows.note: One transaction guarantees no published listing is missing from the bus; idempotent upsert handles bus replays.

options.name: Outbox + CDC indexer (default)
pros: Cannot lose events even on producer crash.
Decouples write commit latency from index latency.
Works the same for any downstream projection (analytics, ML feature store, audit).
cons: Outbox table grows; needs a trim job.
Tail process is one more thing to operate.

options.name: Dual-write: API writes DB + search synchronously
pros: No tail process, no outbox table.
cons: If the search write fails or the API crashes between, the index drifts from the DB.
Couples API latency to search-cluster latency.

failureDrills.scenario: Indexer falls behind by 30 minutes during a 10M-row import.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads still serve the (slightly stale) old documents; backlog drains as the indexer catches up; alerting fires on lag SLO breach.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-partition indexer autoscaling on bus lag; index writes batched; alerts on index_lag > SLO.

failureDrills.scenario: Bad schema change ships and starts breaking documents in the live index.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Alias-swap reverts to the previous index version in seconds; new index is rebuilt cleanly on a fixed schema.
failureDrills.mitigation: Blue/green deploys mandatory for schema changes; never write a breaking change to the live alias.

strong: Names the source of truth explicitly and treats the index as a derived projection.
Uses transactional outbox (or CDC) — not dual writes.
Has blue/green reindexing for schema changes.
weak: Treats the search index as the source of truth.
Dual-writes from the API to DB + search and 'hopes both succeed'.

traps.trap: Reindexing by 'updating the live index in place'
traps.why: A schema bug breaks all reads, and you can't roll back without re-ingesting.
traps.instead: Always build a new index alongside; alias-swap to switch; keep the old index for fast rollback.

recap.before: Approved listings only existed in the DB.
recap.after: Approved listings flow into search with bounded lag; reindexing is a routine, low-risk operation.
recap.newRisk: 50M seeker searches/day now hit the search cluster — caching and read-path scaling are next.

steps.title: 8. Seeker Read Path: CDN-Cached Search, BFF-Composed Detail
description: The public read surface is fronted by a CDN. Search responses are CDN-cached by URL (with vary on query + locale) for short TTLs; listing detail pages and JSON are cached aggressively because they change rarely after publication.

Behind the CDN, a Backend-for-Frontend composes the page: it queries the search index for results, fetches detail rows from the listing DB on cache miss, and stitches in personalization (saved searches, recently viewed) from session state. A query cache in front of the search index absorbs repeated queries (the long tail of 'jobs in berlin' that fires thousands of times).

Critically, the read path has no synchronous dependency on the write pipeline: if the indexer is hours behind, seekers still get the last-known good search results, just slightly stale. This is the non-negotiable: ingestion can degrade; seeker search cannot.

view.caption: CDN absorbs most traffic; BFF composes search + detail; QueryCache absorbs the long tail. Read path has zero synchronous dependency on the write pipeline.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the worst thing that can happen on the seeker read path if the ingestion pipeline is completely down?

concepts.definition: The seeker read path's only dependency on the write pipeline is data freshness — never request-path coupling.
concepts.whyItMatters: Ingestion is bursty, AI/geocoding/fraud have spiky latencies, and any of them can fail. Reads serving 50M searches/day cannot wait for writes.

concepts.definition: A per-surface (web, mobile) service that composes results from search index + DB + personalization in one round trip, returning exactly what the surface needs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Mobile is bandwidth- and latency-constrained; web wants SEO-friendly server-rendered HTML. Separate BFFs let each shape its responses without bloating the others.

concepts.definition: CDN caches at the edge by URL (close to seeker, but per-region). Query cache sits in front of the search index (one per region, shared across BFFs).
concepts.whyItMatters: CDN handles the most popular queries; query cache handles the long tail that varies per session but repeats often enough to matter.

concepts.definition: Serve cached content immediately, refresh asynchronously; new cached value is used for the next request.
concepts.whyItMatters: Even with a 5-minute index lag SLO, seekers don't see the lag — they see fast, slightly older results.

whyNow: Now that there's data to serve, the read path's availability becomes the headline SLO.

options.name: CDN + BFF + query cache (default)
pros: Three layers of caching; most traffic never reaches the search cluster.
BFF lets web and mobile evolve independently.
cons: More moving parts to monitor (CDN, BFF, query cache, index).
Cache invalidation requires care on listing updates.

options.name: Public API hits the search index directly
cons: Every search request hits the cluster; cluster capacity dictates seeker capacity.
No buffer when the indexer or cluster degrades.

failureDrills.scenario: Search index cluster experiences a 10-minute partial outage.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: CDN and query cache continue serving popular queries; only long-tail, uncached queries error.
failureDrills.mitigation: Long CDN TTLs with stale-while-revalidate; BFF returns 'try again' for cache misses, not 5xx; index has multi-AZ replicas.

failureDrills.scenario: Ingestion pipeline halts entirely for 2 hours after an outbox bug.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Seekers see the listings as of 2 hours ago; no errors; alerts fire on index_lag breach.
failureDrills.mitigation: Pipeline replay from outbox once fixed; no read-path code path depends on ingestion liveness.

strong: Names the read/write isolation explicitly: ingestion can degrade, search cannot.
Uses both CDN and query cache (different layers, different hits).
Has separate BFFs (or at least separate response shapes) for web and mobile.
Stale-while-revalidate / soft-fail behavior on cache miss + downstream issues.
weak: BFF or API blocks on synchronous writes / indexing.
One global cache TTL, ignoring that detail pages are far more cacheable than search results.
No plan for what reads do when the index is down.

traps.trap: Caching personalized results at the CDN
traps.why: Edge cache returns one seeker's saved listings to another seeker.
traps.instead: Cache non-personalized search/detail at the CDN; do personalization in the BFF where session is in scope.

recap.before: Listings indexed but no scalable read path.
recap.after: 50M searches/day absorbed by CDN + query cache; index outages are stale-not-5xx.
recap.newRisk: All this works at average load; tail-latency events and noisy tenants in the write pipeline still need handling.

steps.title: 9. Scale, Multi-Tenancy, and Reliability
description: At 50k owners, ingestion is multi-tenant. Partition the event bus by owner_id (composite with listing_id where ordering matters) so one partner's 10M-row drop doesn't head-of-line-block everyone else's writes. A fair scheduler weights worker capacity across owners; per-owner rate limits in the API enforce backpressure at the entry point.

Pipeline stages each scale on their own lag SLO. The DLQ has an operating loop: alert → inspect → fix root cause → replay. Reindexing, replay, and recovery are first-class workflows — runbook-driven, not heroics.

Disaster recovery: the source of truth (listing DB) replicates cross-region; the search index can be rebuilt from the DB anywhere; the raw-file store is the ultimate replay source for ingestion failures.

view.caption: Per-owner partitioning + fair scheduling + per-stage autoscaling + first-class DLQ operations. The whole system fails partially, not totally.

steps.decisionPrompt: One partner pushes a corrupt 10M-row file at the same time another partner does their normal weekly drop. How do you keep both moving?

concepts.definition: Bus partitioning key includes owner_id so one tenant's traffic occupies a bounded subset of partitions.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, a 10M-row drop monopolizes consumer threads and starves every other owner.

concepts.definition: Consumer assigns capacity weighted across owners (and possibly priorities), not strictly partition-round-robin.
concepts.whyItMatters: Static partition assignment can still let a hot owner saturate the workers handling their partitions; the scheduler reallocates.

concepts.definition: Alert on DLQ arrivals → triage → fix root cause → replay (or discard with audit). A DLQ is a worklist, not a graveyard.
concepts.whyItMatters: Most production data loss happens because DLQs filled and got 'cleaned out' instead of replayed.

concepts.definition: Tooling that re-emits a cohort (date range, owner, model version) into a pipeline stage, idempotent against current state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bad enrichment, bad fraud rules, schema changes — all need fast, safe reprocessing without disturbing live writes.

whyNow: The architecture works for the happy path; production is what happens when one component goes wrong AND another is slow.

deepDives.title: Backpressure all the way through
points: API rate-limits per owner (token bucket) — partners get 429 instead of taking down the bus.
Bus partitions per owner — one owner's lag is one owner's lag, not the platform's.
Stages autoscale on per-partition lag, not aggregate lag.
Indexer batches and applies a write-rate ceiling to protect the search cluster.

deepDives.title: Disaster recovery + recovery from bad code
points: Source of truth (listing DB) replicated cross-region; failover documented and rehearsed.
Search index is fully rebuildable from listing DB; raw file store is fully rebuildable from partner uploads.
Every pipeline stage has a 'replay from X' tool, idempotent against current state.

deepDives.title: Observability that maps to SLOs
points: Per-stage lag dashboards (consumer offset vs latest).
Per-owner API usage, error rate, and DLQ rate.
Search index freshness (publish_to_visible histogram) and query latency.
Fraud decision dashboards: FP/FN rates per cohort, model version, rule pack.

strong: Partitions the bus by tenant explicitly, with fair scheduling on top of partitioning.
Treats DLQ as a worklist with an operating loop, not a dead-end.
Names the recovery story (reprocess, reindex, replay) as a first-class workflow.
Maps observability to SLOs the platform commits to (search freshness, index lag).
weak: Assumes scaling is just 'add workers'.
Has no per-tenant isolation; one bad partner can take down ingestion.
DLQ alerts but nobody owns the loop to actually process them.

traps.trap: Skipping per-tenant partitioning because 'we're not that big yet'
traps.why: The first 10M-row file from a single tenant takes down the whole pipeline; refactoring under pressure is far worse than designing it in from the start.
traps.instead: Make tenant partitioning the default; even at low scale it costs almost nothing.

recap.before: Single-tenant happy path, no recovery story.
recap.after: Per-tenant isolation, autoscaled stages, DLQ + replay as ops workflows, search reads always-on.
recap.newRisk: More partitions and lanes to operate; routing keys, hot-partition rebalancing, and per-stage SLOs become explicit owners' responsibilities.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Listing Platform
finalDesign.description: External systems push listings via authenticated APIs (accept-fast → bus) or drop files into per-owner SFTP directories (file watcher → streaming parser → bus); owners and admins use the same APIs through an admin panel with AI-assisted authoring. The shared event bus is partitioned per owner. A pipeline of independently-scaling stages — normalize, enrich (geocode/classify/translate/score), dedup (exact then fuzzy with blocking + ANN), and fraud (rules + ML + human review) — writes the listing DB. A transactional outbox + indexer projects listings into a sharded search index with blue/green reindexing. Seekers hit a CDN-cached BFF that reads from the search index and listing DB. The read path has zero synchronous dependency on the write pipeline.

view.caption: All ingress (FTP, API, manual + AI) converges on a partitioned event bus. Independent pipeline stages (normalize, enrich, dedup, fraud) write the listing DB. Outbox + CDC drive the search indexer (blue/green). Seekers hit a CDN-cached BFF over the search index and listing DB — fully decoupled from the write pipeline.

flows.name: End-to-end: partner POST → seeker visible
flows.note: Async pipeline behind the bus; outbox guarantees the indexer never misses a publish; reads have no synchronous dependency on writes.

functional.requirement: FTP imports for bulk listings (CSV/XML/JSON, possibly compressed)
functional.how: Per-owner SFTP → object-storage landing zone → streaming parser → row events on the bus. Same downstream pipeline as the API.

functional.requirement: API for create / update / partial-update / delete / bulk
functional.how: Owner API authenticates, rate-limits per owner, idempotently publishes to the bus; bulk returns a job id with status + webhook.
Admin panel uses the same Owner API; the AI Assistance Service serves interactive title/category/policy suggestions with provenance.

functional.requirement: Listing enrichment (geocode, classify, translate, quality, completeness)
functional.how: Enrichment orchestrator runs independent enrichers in parallel; outputs are versioned and separable from owner-provided fields.

functional.requirement: Duplicate detection across owners and channels
functional.how: Exact dedup on (owner_id, external_id); fuzzy dedup with blocking + ANN; borderline → moderator queue.

functional.requirement: Fraud prevention with rules + ML and human review
functional.how: Hot-reloadable rule packs + ML scoring with feature store; auto-publish low risk, hold-for-review borderline, auto-reject high risk; decisions logged.
Transactional outbox + CDC + partitioned indexer with batched upserts; blue/green reindexing for schema evolution.
CDN-cached BFF reads search index + listing DB; query cache absorbs long-tail searches; stale-while-revalidate.

functional.requirement: Admin panel for owners and platform admins (RBAC)
functional.how: Admin UI hits the same APIs with owner-scoped or admin-scoped tokens; every action audited.

nonFunctional.requirement: Seeker reads always-available even when ingestion is degraded
nonFunctional.how: Read path depends only on the search index + listing DB; no synchronous link to the write pipeline.
Outbox-driven indexing with per-partition autoscaling on bus lag; index lag is an SLO with alerting.
Idempotency-Key dedupes producer retries; (owner_id, external_id) unique constraint dedupes logical listings across channels.
Per-owner partitioning on the bus, per-owner rate limits at the API, fair scheduling across consumer groups.

nonFunctional.requirement: Auditability of every state change and fraud / dedup decision
nonFunctional.how: Audit log table; fraud_decisions and duplicate_candidates capture inputs + outputs + versions; outbox preserves event stream.

nonFunctional.requirement: Recoverability (bad import, bad model, bad rules)
nonFunctional.how: Raw-file store + outbox + replay tools per stage; blue/green search reindex; reprocessing is a first-class workflow.

say: Confirm the dominant tension: high-throughput multi-tenant writes feeding a low-latency always-available read surface.
Pin down scale: 10M active / 500M historical / 50k owners / 50M searches per day / 5-min freshness target.
Ask: how strict is read availability when ingestion is degraded? (Answer: it's the headline SLO.)
Walk through the synchronous create-and-index design.
Enumerate concretely what fails: 10M-row FTP files, slow geocoder, indexer outage, noisy partner.
Decouple: API as accept-fast, publish to a partitioned event bus.
Add FTP path that converges on the same row events.
Async pipeline: normalize → enrich (parallel) → dedup (exact then fuzzy) → fraud (rules + ML + human).
Source-of-truth listing DB + outbox + indexer; blue/green reindex.
CDN-cached BFF for seeker reads; isolate reads from writes.
DLQ operating loop and replay tooling.
Disaster recovery: DB cross-region, index rebuildable, raw files preserved.
Trade-offs: where they'd push back on synchronous vs async; where humans must be in the loop.
What they'd measure: index lag, DLQ depth, FP/FN per cohort, per-owner API error rate.

expectations: Names the source of truth and that search is a derived projection.
Knows POST should be 202 + async; can sketch a basic queue + worker pipeline.
Identifies that deduplication and fraud are 'a thing' but doesn't need full strategies.
Picks a real pattern for each pipeline stage (Outbox/CDC, blocking + ANN, rules + ML).
Discusses idempotency at both the HTTP and listing-content layer.
Explains read-path caching layers and what each absorbs.
Explicitly defends read/write isolation as the headline SLO.
Designs per-tenant partitioning + fair scheduling from the start, not 'later'.
Treats DLQ as a worklist with an operating loop; designs replay as a first-class workflow.
Discusses fraud-model degradation policy, blue/green reindex rollback, and a recovery story for each pipeline stage.
Connects FP/FN measurement, owner reputation features, and moderator labeling into a feedback loop.
Talks about cost: AI/geocoding budgets, search-cluster sizing per region, raw-file retention.
Designs the system's failure modes by tenant cohort, not as a single platform.
Has an explicit story for safe rollouts of new enrichment models / fraud rules / index schema.

followUps: How would your design change if FTP imports contained 100M rows (10x our peak)?
A partner sends the same logical listing through FTP yesterday and API today — walk through every layer that prevents a duplicate.
Rebuild the entire search index across all listings without downtime — how?
A bug marks 1M listings as duplicates. How do you recover, and how do you keep audit + provenance intact?
How would you safely roll out a new fraud-scoring model without surprising trust-and-safety?
How would you let an owner see exactly why a specific listing was rejected, including the model version that scored it?
Multiple listing domains (jobs, real estate, vehicles) have very different schemas — how do you generalize without losing domain-specific fields?
Prevent search-result scraping without breaking legitimate seeker traffic.
Design the admin audit log so it's queryable, tamper-evident, and not the primary write bottleneck.
Make seeker search 50% cheaper — what's the first thing you'd cut?
